title: Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study

learningObjectives.text: Explain requirements and feature planning using a new example and justify the result.
Trace and explain user stories and flowcharts using a new example and justify the result.
Explain architecture planning using a new example and justify the result.
Explain project retrospective using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use planning a web project — mapty case study without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Project Planning: Mapty** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Requirements and feature planning** → **User stories and flowcharts** → **Architecture planning** → **Project retrospective**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Requirements and feature planning
sections.body: Start with user-visible outcomes and convert them into explicit behavior, inputs, edge cases, and acceptance conditions. Break large capabilities into end-to-end slices that can be implemented and verified independently.

sections.title: User stories and flowcharts
sections.body: User stories capture who needs a capability and why; flowcharts expose sequence and decisions. Together they reveal missing states before code is written.

sections.title: Architecture planning
sections.body: Architecture planning identifies data, responsibilities, boundaries, and communication paths. The goal is to reduce costly ambiguity about ownership and change, not predict every future file.

sections.title: Project retrospective
sections.body: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study connects Requirements and feature planning, User stories and flowcharts, Architecture planning.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: Apply Requirements and feature planning deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study is needed.

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study?

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Requirements and feature planning, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study?

choices.label: Avoid Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: Use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
